CURRENT AFFAIRS,

We were not present at the recent shareholders’ meet-
ing of the Midland Great Western Railway, but we
read the Chairman’s speech in a daily paper. It was
a good speech, and we think that the Hon. Richard A.
Nugent deserves to be congratulated upon it. His abti-
tude towards the shareholders and the public as dis-
closed in his remarks, is calculated to evoke sympathy

" from both classes. Unlike another Railway Chairman,
he did not attempt to bluff the public by asserting that
he was a ‘ gentleman;’’ he rested content to act like
one. After all, there is great healing power in the
man who plays the gentleman in strong contrast with
the irritant element in the bumptious bounder who
shocks an unbelieving and critical public by blatantly
screaming that he is one. It takes even morc than tan
hoots and a title to make that rather indefinable, though
very palpable, thing that we call a gentleman.  There
was no “side ’’ on the Midland Chairman’s remarks.

. Wo trust that tho railway over which ha presides
with dignity is taking full advantage at Tuam, Clax
morris and Athlone of the sentiment which the ant

Trish attitude of the Great Sick and Sourface Rail-
way’s dealings with parcels addressed in Irish at Tuam
has callod forth. We trust that the Midland -Great
Western Railway authorities will see their way at once
to include Irish as an obligatory subject for their com-
petitive examinations for clerkships. Irish has come to
stay ; and placing the matter on no higher plane than
business interests, we put it to Mr. Nugent that in view
genorally of the movement in furtherance of the Irish
language, and in view particularly of the struggle at
Tuam, he would be doing a good and sound business
stroke for his company if he announced that Irish was
in future to be an obligatory subject for examinations
for candidates for clerkships.  Excursions and other
functions in direct connection with the Irish Language
Movement involve in terms of money a considerable
amount. We suggest that a revenue of, say, £5,00¢
could be easily diverted to any of the three great rail-
ways of Ireland that, by a graceful concession to the
Irish Language Movement, made a bid—a business bid
if you like—for the preferential custom of those in-
terested in the revival of the Irish language.

We put it to Mr. Nugent and his fellow-directors that
if they placed Irish on their examination programme
that they would—not so much for love of them as for z
wish, and an honest wish, to hif the other railways that
stood out against Irish—that they would arouse a real
tendency to so arrange excursions and other functions
in connection with the Irish Language Movement as
to benefit the Midland Great Western Railway con-
siderably.

In the course of the Chairman’s speech, he said:—
“ Our brick traffic has steadily increased, and the bulk
of it is from Kingscourt, an industry on our railway
which enjoys low rates from us for its produce, as we
are glad to do all we can to assist in developing the
industry, which, T regret to say, is one of the very few
to be found on our system. I may mention that a peat
fuel and peat moss litter manufactory has been estab-
lished close to our station at Inny Junction. It was
slarted under the auspices of the Department of Agri-
culture, and, at their request, we put down a siding far
the traffic, and granted specially reduced rates, which,
we trust, will secure its success. It is not necessary, 1
think, to tell you that your directors are at.all times
ready and willing to co-operate with that Department,
or with anyone else, for the purpose of developing the
1esources of the country, or of fostering any new in-
dustries which may be in contemplation.’’

We applaud the spivit of these remarks. They con-
tain the spirit of sound business; and people who may
contemplate starting new industries will naturaily be
encouraged by the expression of these views to Favour-
ably consider districts served by the Midland Great:
Western Railway,

Wo have received the following official communica-
tion : —Catholic Defence Society.—The usual monthly
meeting of thoe Council of the Catholic Defence Society
was held on Tuesday, the 6th inst., when important
business was transacted. A large number of gentle-
men were proposed for membership, and admitted te
tho socicty. The treasurer’s statement showed a sub-
stantial balance to credit in bank, and further subscrip-
tions were handed in.  The Council adjourned after
making arrangements for the general meeting of the
members to be held in the evening. A large mesting
of the members was held in the Antient Concent Rooms
at 8 o’clock. The secretary read a very interesting re-
port of the progress made since the socicty was formed.

Several matters of interest to Catholics were discussed,
and arrangements made for the extension of the work
in Dublin and throughout the country.

Most  of our veaders, wec are sure, will be
gl:?d to hear of the progress and activity of
this much-needed and beneficent society. It stands
for the assertion in this country of Tolerance,
Justice, and Fair Play; it secks to invade no man’s
rights, but it lives to attempt to assert the civic and
economic rights of the people of his country who happen
to be “Idolators.” It is ncedless for us to say any-
tluugiby way of explanation for the crying need of this
combination ; facts, which are stated in almost every
issue of the LEADER since its establishment, cry out aloud
for such a movement for the establishmont of Justice
and Fair Play, and a national working Tolerance. We
have scen the Orange Society at work in the notorious
Tyrone case in which the man Coote was the « hero;”’
the Freemasons ave an old and going concern working
along the line of well-established Ascendancy. Though
we have the temerity to believe that we may be as far-
seeing as most people, yet we confess we cannot pro-
phesy at any given time what may happen even in the
next twenty-four hours; perhaps the happy time may
come when the need of the Catholic Defence Society
will cease. and when at a conference (blessed word!) the
officers of the Catholic Defence Society, of the Free and
Accepted Masons in Ireland, of the Orangemen, “In-
dependent >’ or otherwise, may agree to disband their
several forces. ~We have no authority, of cowrse, to
speak on’ behalf of the Catholic Defence Society, but
when the Orangemen offer to disband, and the Free-
masons determine to end their organisation in Ireland,
we think that the Catholic Defence Society would sym-
pathetically consider a proposal that it also should
cease. However, that is not a matter of practical
politics just yet.

In this imperfect world of to-day the Catholic De-
fence Society is a necessity. We trust that it will grow
and flourish, and that under wise and firm manage-
ment it will make its beneficent power felt in every
dark corner in the land where bigotry and intolerance
are doing their work.

The February number of The New Ireland Review
opens with an interesting article, “ Half Way to Home
Rule,”” by Mr. Arthur Synan. Amongst the book re-
views is a critical notice of “Tom O’Kelly.””  The
writer having made some general remarks on vulgarity
and the class in which it is usually found, proceeds:—

We have said that this type is not wanting in Irish society.
We might go further, and say that in Irish saciety it is unusually
ludicrous, and unusually offensive. The. standard to which the
Ivish novus homo strives to conform is, for the reasons alrcady
ziven, usually English, Superior English ways, when aped by
pretentious Englishmen are laughable and no more—witness the
“ Kickleburye on the Rhine,” or the ‘“Book of Snobs.” But in
Ireland these pretentious efforts are more than ludicrous; they
offend the national sonse of the fitness of Irish ways for Irish
people; and when the Irish satirist takes these -offenders in
hands, he deals with him in more severo fashion than did M.
Thackeray with his Kickleburys and his Snobs.

Mr. D. P. Moran is a satirist, an Irish satir'st, onc of the most
vigorous representatives of tho literary school to which he be-
longe. In his book juet published, “Tom O'Kelly,” he sets him-
self to castigate that type of vulgarity which we have described
as specifically Irish. “Tom O'Kelly” is not a novel in tho
accepted sense of that term. The horo’s course is singularly un-
oventful, he has not a single hair-breadth cscape in his carcor,
nor are his adventures such as should ond conventionally in a
happy marriage. It will soon dawn upon tho roador of tho hook
that we are introduced to Master Tom merely in order that
through him we may make acquaintance with other membors of
his family, and with the society of Ballytown in which they
move. Ballytown is not to be found on the map; the name is
employed to symbolise what Mr. Moran calls ‘“the cesenco of
highly respectable Anglo-Trish urban life,” rather than to serve
26 a place-name. Should anyone seck to locate it geographically
Mr. Moran eoxplains for his or her guidance: “Dear Madam or
Sir, there is at least one Ballytown in nearly cvery county in
Treland, and in most counties there are sevorsl of them.'

The review of the book ends up in this way:—

This and much more of $ho same character presonts us with
rather a pitiable aspect of Ir'sh life. Is it the best way to cure

the defects here oxposed to ridicule them? On this there may, as
Mr. Moran intimates in his concluding chapter, bo two opinions.
o holds his own strongly, and as strongly acts on it. The
Fronch provorb tells us that ridicule Kkills. If it can IELL the ‘evil
things against whioh Mr. Moran dirccts his shafts: we may put
up with the incidental inconvenicnces which accompany it. If the
lash fuils of its offect in this case the failure cannot be set. down
to uny want of vigour in Mr. Moran's application of tho instru-
went.

Amongst the other articles in 7'he New Ireland Review
is “ Dan——An Irish Peasant,”” by Mrs. Norah Tynan

O’Mahony.

Tho list of the Vice-Presidents of the Irish Association
for the Prevontion of Intemperance is not uninteresting.
The names of tho Executive Committee are placed in
alphabetical order, but with regard to the Vice-Presi-
donts thero is evidently an attempt at an order of pre-
cedence. The list of Vice-Presidents is headed by the
Protestant Archbishop of Armagh! Thero are four arch-
bishops altogether amongst the list, two Catholics and
two Protestants. Of course, as luck would have it—the
“saved ’ in these little matters always have luck ' on
their sido—a Protestant archbishop is placed first—no
doubt by mere accident, as the paid secretary, Mr. J. B.
Moriarty, notwithstanding his name, happens to be of
the “saved,”’ and not what a contributor in the Zrish
Temperance League Jowrnal might call a believer in
“ the sacerdotal nostrums and indescribable mummeries
of the Church of Rome.”” After the four archbishops
and before all the bishops, both “ Saved ”’ and “ Idola-
trous,”’ comes the Moderator of the General Assembly.
When we come to the heads of educational institutions
we find—by accident, no doubt—that the notorious bigot
Anthony Traill, of the Stale-herring University, takes
precedence to ths low-down President of Maynooth Col-
loge, where the “surpliced ruffians’’ come from, the
Very Rev. Dr. Mannix. Amongst the Right Hons. the
blatant Union Jackeen, the Earl of Meath, takes first
place, The O’Conor Don, a “ Tame,”” no doubt,
an “ Idolator,”’ takes second last place. We - irrow-
fully believe that a great number of England’s Waithful
on, who pretend to take an interest in Temperance
oform, would rather see Treland drunk than Ireland
Catholic and National.

The:e was a feast of raimeis and other things at the
Dolphin Hotel on Saturday night. Some eminent gen-
tlemen are boiling over with *‘toleration '’ just now.
Evideutly raimeis has only been skotched, not killed,
and we are still almost as green a people as ever. A
Nationalist represented West Belfast twenty years ago:
subsequently tho seat was lost, and this year owing to a
three-cornered fight a Nationalist got in by a mere fluke.
These are the facts—but low-down facts have
their vicissitudes, too, and who so mean-spirited as to
do them reverence at the Dolphin Hotel on Saturday
night? On that occasion  Travesty on the Finale to an
Ttalian opera’’ was, according to the Freeman’s
Jowrnal, discoursed by the Glee Singers, even though no
song in Irish, not to say in the traditional style, violated
the green digestions of diners who had partaken of such
entrees as “ Kromeskys a la Russe,”” “ mutton cutlets a
I'Ttalienne,’”’ and at which one of the soups was
called “ Consomme Victoria.”’ Why, we wonder, was
not one of the “ Releves ’’ called “ Albert Edward 2"

Now i is contended that but for about 200 Protes-
tants in the enormous constituency of West Belfast that
notwithstanding the 150 votes polled by Mr. Carlisle
the Unionist would have got in, and the Nationalist
would have failed to slip in by his microscopic majority
of 16. This is, let us point out, not a fact, it is only
a contention—a different matter.  According to Mr.
Devlin the Nationalists, who twenty years ago wereiin a
majority, wero this year nearly 600 behind on the regis-
ber. Carlisle polled 150, and so there was soms 400 majo-
rity on the register to account for. Now there are vari-
ous ways of accounting for Mr. Devlin’s majority of 16.

The calculation: of about 600 Unionist majority. may.

have been incorrect ; a larger proportion of Nationalists
than Unionists may have gone to the poll. But when
the “tolerance-proving ”’ microbe is working such ex-

planations as these are scornfully brushed aside. Mr.
Devlin said : —* There still remained a majority against
me of well over four hundred, and I must, therefore,
in the natural order of things, have received over two
hundred Protestant votes in order to win the election
(hear, hear). I think that is a very remarkable thing
when you find two hundred men, not remaining away,
but changing over to the programme and the policy of a
man whose declarations were clear and .defined.” As
‘We are not suffering from the ‘“tolerance-proving’’
disease, let us point out that Mr, Devlin’s “ therefore in
the natural order of things’’ is by no means convincing.
But even if we were to be so complaisant with these
post-prandial orators, and allow that the contention that
two hundred Protestants of this enormous West Belfast
constituency voted for the Nationalist was a fact, what
does it signify? Do a few hundred of the Charles E.
Martin type of Cawtholic Unionist in South Dublin or
Stephen’s Green Division indicate a great going over
tendency to Unienism and England’s Faithful Garrison
on the part of the “ true blue "’ Papists of these consti-
tuencies? We suppose even Orangemen and Protestant
“true blue "’ circles have their “ tames '’ as well as we
have them on our side. Now, accepting the contention
that 200 Protestants voted for Mr. Devlin as a fact, is
that fact any adequate cause for the flare up of “ toler-
ance-proving " that it has apparently brought about?

Mr. John Dillon, who never raised his voice at a gene:
ral meeting of the Great Northern Bigot Railway in pro-
test at the treatment of Catholic employés and Catholics
who, ““in the natural order of things,”’ as Mr. Devlin
might phrase it, would be in the service of
that railway, rose to rare heights of raimeis. Mr.
Dillon wanted to know “ How can we restore the ancient
spirit of our people while the dead hand of the foreigner
is on the throat of our race.”” We may politely tell Mr.
Dillon that if the hand of the foreigner that is on the
throat of our race was a ““ dead hand "’ it would be all
right for us; unhappily the foreign hand is a very
“live ”” one. Mr. J. Dillon’s speech reminded us of an
anachronism, if we may put it that way; it reminded
us of a gramophone twenty years old. The politicians
it appears are “in reality the vanguard of all work.”
Are they? What vanguard work did they do as politi-
cians for the Industrial Revival, for the cause of ‘Toler-
ance, Justice and Fair Play, for the Irish Language
Movement? We have struggled to place politicians as
such in their place, and Mr. Dillon ought to have sense
enough now to understand that this coupling of politi-
cians with vanguards only tends to exile hostility to the
claims and pretensions of men of his kind, and in no way
smoothes the way for harmonious co-operation. Mr.
Dillon, who never raised his voice at a general meeting
of the Great Northern Railway, declared that “ Mr.
Devlin could not have won West Belfast except by the
votes of a considerable body of the Orange workingmen
of Belfast.”” Mr. Dillon probably prefers to be flattex-
ing a few Orangemen than bearding blatant bigotry in
the boardroom of the Great Northern Railway.

The contention that Mr. Devlin fluked into West
Belfast—where Mr. Sexton had years ago gone in—by
the combined force of a third candidate and 200 Protes-
tant: voters is, it appears, the “ first impression *’ made
on the Orange democracy. We thought we had heard
of Orange and Green and attempts to unite them before!
However, Mr. Dillon said:—“ And I hail to-night in
Mr. Dovlin and tho Belfast men the men who have
made the first improssion on the Orange democracy of
the North.”

Could any of our readers give us a full list of tho Nut-
ting scholars to the Stale-herring University? We have
heard a whisper that all the “ Tdolatrous”’ ‘youngsters
that got an offer of theso bribes have sneaked into Trinity
College, but we have no authoritative information on tho
point. We think that all the ““ spirited *’ lads who have
gone, or who have been sont by their stalwart parents,
up tho back stairs of the Souper University deserva famo.
‘We would like a full list or nothing, for we would not

care to make only an odd youngster famous. A full
list of the Nutting blacklegs would be interesting and
wholesome. If the struggle for University rights for
Irish Catholics is not a sham the blacklegs who have
taken Nutting’s bribes must be shown up no matter
who thoy are and no matter in what Catholic colleges
they have been  educated.”

The mow famous book, Catholicity and Progress in
Ireland, by our distinguished contributor “ M. O’R.,”
has gone into a third edition. Some notes are added,
and former typographical errors corrected. ~We hear
that already nearly half this third edition—though it is
only out for a few wecks—has been sold.

Our “tolerant’’ contemporary, the Belfast Neus
Letter, has reviewed ‘“ Tom O’Kelly.” True, it is a
brief review, but those of our readers who have read the
book may be amused by it:— This is an Irish story,
and it is mainly concerned with the O’Kelly family.
Love and politics are mingled in an entertaining picture
of social life in certain circles in this country. It is
noteworthy that the word ‘ Protestant ’ is used through-
out the book to designate generally  the foreign racial
element in Ireland.” It is not so much a story as an
anti-English and anti-Protestant manifesto.””

A correspondent says that a question which frequently
crops up when the Irish drink question is under discus-
sion is whether the £13,816,318 spent on drink is the
“duty "’ on the liquor made in the country, or the money
paid for drink in the country. The figure is an approxi-
mate calculation of the amount spent on drink—duty in-
cluded—by the people of this country. We have never
examined the methods of calculation, but we believe
that the calculation is very carefully made. We assume
that it is made on the strength of what we may call
honest drink consumed in the country, and therefore we
have always suspected that the figures are somewhat
under the mark in view of the amount of adulteration
by other materials besides water that Mr. Bung is sus-
pected of sometimes adding to the drink he vends. We
have all heard of Fair Day whiskey.

The recent harangue of the Ardee parson, the Rev.
Canon Locket Forde, to which we called attention, has
aroused great indignation in Ardee and other parts
where the parson is known. We see that a contributor
of our contemporary, the Dundalk Democrat, breaks out
into verse in last week’s issue. We quote one of the five
stanzas: —

“ We'll make this Parson eat his words,
Or know the reason why;
His base attempts to libel us
We fearlessly defy.
Too long by ranters such as he
Our country’s been bedaubed,
Our race insulted and defamed,
Our prestige from us robbed.

We take the following from the leading article in the
paper run by the ¢ Grand Master * of the “Indepen-
dent >’ Orangemen, one Mr., Crawford, recently hailed
by Mr. Willie Redmond as a Liberal Protestant ™’

It is lamentable to find so-cailed Protestant papers in this
country protesting against any altoration of the Education Act
of 1902, and it is still more doplorablo to find Protestant electors
voting for men who supported .tlmt moasure, Tt is absurd to
suggest that the quostion has no interest for Irish Protestants. It
Vitally concorns us, not only ns affecting the futuro of Protestant
Tingland, but also as cstablishing precedonts in the matter of
oducation which cannot but influence the trend of logislation in
tho sottloment of tho Irish education problem. What Parliament
dooides to-day for England will bo followed to-morrow for Ireland.

THore is a pretty story that a correspondent sends us—
“ Qomo of the Bunegs in Cahirciveen said in reference to
the Anti-Troating League that they could mot live at
all if the League was & success, for how could they be
giving ¢ change’ to every individual who wanted a drink
~—a most weighty reason, truly!”

By the way, as we are referring to the Anti-Treating
League we may mention another story that reaches us.
The Anti-Treating League was started in a certain
parish twelve months ago, but on the first Fair Day in
the market town of the parish the Anti-Treaters were
laughed at for calling for their own drinks. 1t appears
they of the town had not heard at that time of the Anti-
Treating League. The moral of this story is that if the
League is to be a success it should be started simultane-
qusly in, if not eveiy parish in the country—unough
why not?—at least in every parish within certain well-
defined areas. This view no longer rests on speculation,
for we have the experience of the movement in the Clon-
fert diocese to refer back to.

The annual concert in aid of the Catholic Boys’ Bri-
gade, Church Street, will be held in the Round Room,
Rotunda, on Monday evening, February 19th. Soveral
well-known singers will contribute, including the popu-
tar tenor, Mr. Patrick O’Shea.

‘We need hardly remind our many readers who will be

ding shamrock to friends abroad that an Irish manu-

ctured box is the most appropriate covering for it.

Dublin, have as usual manufactured a special stock of

The secrstary of the Liverpool Catholic Digcesan As-
sociaticn, which speaks officially for a Catholic popula-
tion of 400,000 and for Catholic schools to the number
of 167, with 80,000 Catholic children on the rolls, writes
us:—“ The Association met to-day and passed unani-
mously the following resolution—‘ That this meeting
views with indignation and alarm expressions made use
of by the Minister of Education, from which may be
inferred an intention to convert all the Elementary
Schools of the country into Nonconformist Schools.” I
have been directed to communicate the resolution to

»

you.

We see that our old friend, Captain Wade-Thompson,
the Pervert Priests’ Protector, is treated to a biographi-
cal sketch illuminated by a photographic reproduction
in the current issue of the Church of England’s Faith-
ful Garrison Gazette. The Pervert Priests’ Protector,
judging by his picture, has a very receding forehead.
We are not surprised to read that the Protector is a
graduate of the University .where the stale herrings
come from. He is a B.L. too, but—perhaps, nezdless
to say—he never practised ; he turned his idle moments
ta account, by joining the Dublin Militia! Fe is a Free-
mason .as well as Pervert Priests’ Protector, and also
Senior Trustee of the “ Achill Mission Estate.”” We are
told-that “ he is a very definite Protestant of the ‘ Low ’
8chool;”” and also that “In no instance have his cour-
age and thoroughness done more conspicuous service to
Irish. Churchmen and loyalists than in connection with
his exposure of the notorious ‘ Catholic Association ’ in
1903.” Strangely enough, there is no memtion in this
biographical sketch of this eminent Freemason, briefless
barrister and commissioned militiaman of his service as
a Pervert Priests’ Protector.

The Language Movement does not appear to be thriv-
ing in the town of Ballybay, in the .County of
Monaghan. It started some months ago with great pro-
mise, and now it has apparently fizzled out; there is
neither a meeting-room nor a teacher. According to an
article in our contemporary, the I’mz_:lc’a Advocate, there
appears to have been a lot of bickering and story-carry-
ing concerning what it styles “ the rise and fall of the
Trish movement in Ballybay.”” ~ This failure is very
much to be regretted, for Ballybay is an _important
centre, and it should have, at least, one thriving branch
of the Gaelic League.

A Voice From TEXAS.

El Paso, Texas, Jan. 25th, 1906.
Dear Sir,—Travelling some years ago on horseback
through one of the loneliest and most desolate parts of
Colorado, T was lucky enough, before night came on, to
strike a trail which led to a sheep herder’s camp. I
entered the log shanty and started to prepare supper.
I found a lantern and teok a look around the hut. Ima-
gine my surprise to find two dozen copies of the LEADER
beginning with the first issue. Presently the herder
came in, carrying a long rifle on one shoulder and a
couple of wolf skins on the other. ~He was an Irish-
man and had been herding sheep in that country for
fourteen years. ~ He' was an ardent believer in the
Lraper.and its policy. This little incident shows that
in the few short ycars of its existence the fame of the
=R has reached even the remote parts of the world.
add that the Leaper has been my greatest conso-
lation since its first copy reached me. :
It has awakenad a new spirit and a mew hope in the
Irish race. I conclude by wishing success to the LEADER
in its noble efforts to Irishize Ireland. - The signs of con-
alescence are to-day apparent in a land which, in pre-
Rr days, was truly sick unto death,—Yours faith-.

P. F. Crongsssy.

DQUGLAS HYDE'S AMERICAN MISSION.

HAT may be the permanent effect of Dr. Douglas
Hyde’s great mission to the United States!
An  Cpaobin sent us a few days ago the menu card of
the complimentary banquet given in his honour at the
Hotel Jefferson by the United Irish Societies of St
Louis on January 24th. The toasts at the banquet were
as follows:—“ Our Guest,”” “Irish Ireland,”’ * The
United States of America,”” “Erin’s Exiles,”” “ The
President of the United States,” and “ The Sea-Divided
Gael.”” On the same page as the toast list, and over
which Dr. Hyde pencils: —“péa¢ é peo!” we read:—
‘“ Had the Sea-Divided Gael carried their language with
them in their wanderings what a potent imperial race
the Irish would be to-day.’—From The Philosophy of
Lrish Ireland, by D. P. Moran.™

An Trish-speaking Tmperial race must now, no doubt,
be regarded as a Might Have Been ; the seeds of it should -
have been sown every day since the famine exodus. But
they were not, and so the millions of men of Irish de-
scent soattered over America and other countries have
lost their language, and we may as well reconcile our-
selves to the fact that they will never regain it. But all
is not lost; and the quotation on the menu card of the
United Irish Societies of St. Louis indicates that the
practical mind of the Irish American grasps at once the
federating power of the mother tongue. The question
is, and it is & most important question, how much re-
mains possible for the Irish in America to do for the
Trish at home? To what extent might the spread of the
language amongst the Irish in America be made a force
for the benefit of Ireland? The battles of Ireland must
for the most part be fought out in Ireland, and generally
we look upon exiles in America and elsewhere as lost to
Treland unless they intend to come back. When we
take into consideration what a powerful absorbing en-
tity America is, The prospect of the up-keep of a per-
manent distinctive Irish element in America when ab-
normal emigration ceases strikes us as rather poor. Those
of Irish extraction who stand steadfast by the Catholic
faith they brought from Ireland may keep together, but
not necessarily as an Irish-American force. The spread
of the Irish language even now in the United States
would help somewhat to perpetuate the Irish element: -
there, and that tendency will be one of the chief perma-
nent results of Douglas Hyde’s mission.

This brings us to the interesting question—How might
Treland benefit by a strong Irish language development
in America? Up to this Ireland has been contributing
all to America, and getting nothing.back; so much so
indeed that some years ago we wrote an article askive -

430

whether England or America was the greater drawback
to Ireland, and we gave our verdict against Americy.

America drains Ireland of many thousands of emi-
grants annually ; these emigrants she gets fr:gd nf%\‘;lbhd-
standing’ that poor over-taxed .a.nd rack-ren [relan
has to bear the cost of rearing them to man’s and
woman'’s state, and a large proportion of them also take
each a certain sum of money with them out of the coun-
try. The natural emigration—natural under our abnor-
mal circumstances—is greatly intensified by the prepaid
passage money. What do we get from America In re-
twn? Practically nothing. But if America gets freo
gifts in emigrants that Ircland has defrayed the expenso
of rearing, her ports are guarded by high tariffs against
such industrial goods as wo could send her in the way of
trade. It is only by a radical disarrangement of all
perspective that tho personally very genorous subscrip-
tions that individuals in America yearly give in support
of political and other movements in Ireland can be made
to appear of any importance from a national point of
view. After all even £30,000 in subscriptions would
only—-in terms of money—compensate for 150 full-grow::
free emigrants, if we value each emigrant at the modest
figure of £200 each.

If our manufactures are met with high tariff walls at

American ports the American hoot manufacturers and
the agricultural machine makers are fres to dump, and
do dump, their wares on our markets in competition with
the wares of our hard pressed manufacturers at home.
We believe it is claimed that the Irish political influence
in America prevents the Anglo-American treaty, and
that that power raises Ireland politically in the calcula-
tions of British statesmen. Be that as it may, the pre-
venting of such a treaty does not prevent emigration, or
promote industries, or open the markets of America
freely to Irish goods.
The Irish in America could help Ireland by sending
over some capital and also some of their captains of in-
dustry to promote manufacturing enterprises here ; they
could help her by making a market for Irish goods in
America either in face of the tariffs, or, better still, if
possible, using their political influence to procure pre-
ferential rates for Irish goods. We badly want outlets
Tor the industrial products of this country, and so far as
we can see America up to this has been very little use to
us; she has provided an outlet not for the industrial
products of Treland, but a gaping outlet for emigration
—for the people who, if Ireland is to be perpetuated as
the mother country of the Sea-Divided Gael, should be
working at home here. The interest in the Irish lan-
guage movement, which Douglas Hyde with signal vigour
is arousing all over the United States, will no doubt act
as a great moral force on the side of the movement at
home, and as much of that force as we can get is badly
wanted over here. The making of a scattered Imperial
race of the Sea-Divided Gael, including a few little Irish
speaking republics in various parts of the world, was
possible fifty years ago. Alas, we are no longer capable
of sending out millions of Irish speaking people across
the wide surface of the world, and so that dream of
empire is now a might have been.

The existing play of ordinary American and Irish
American forces does much against Ircland. The plain
case has never been persistently put to Trish America.
Soma of us are no doubt too “ideal >’ and “ spiritual ”’
‘1o mention the low down word trade or commerce to our
exiled brothers. That tragi-comic “pride of race,”
that is said to be the curse of Clonakilty, is too strong in
us to hint to our Irish American friends that we too, a
struggling nation though we arve, must needs march,
like an army, on our stomachs, and that sentiment and
sympathy, though good in their own way, are not as
nourishing as potatoes and milk. What we would like
to say to Trish America is—Your affection we very much
esteem, but to come to business, what can you do in
everyday tangible ways for this dead and alive nation
that a remnant of the race that mothered you are trying
to nurture back to health and vigour? There are no
people, we feel gure, who would recognise” sooner and
more 1'.0‘“1“'7' sympathise with this sensible attitude than
~ the Trish Americans. Irish American sympathy is very

pormanent, forces that work day-and night in the direc-
tion of the life and the death of the race as a distinct

in the ocean.

‘What Ireland wants with regard to Irish-America is
some permanent arrangements that will set free a play
of forces that will result in the Irish in America being

MR. DAYITT ON THE EDOCATION QUESTION.—I{

N Tk Leaper of the week before last, T made some
I observations on Mr. Davitt’s letter to the Freeman.
From what I said, the public can sce that he who lec-
tures them on the English Education Question has him-
self much to learn. His letter struck me as the letter
of one who listened with docility to the Nonconformists,
and took in all they told him. I should like to pursue
this subject a little farther.

I am not writing my name at the end. ~ What the
public who read what Mr. Davitt or I write want to
know, is—not who are Mr. Davitt and I, but what is
the convincing value of our words. I deal with M.
Davitt’s words only; I have nothing to say to the man
standing behind his words. If I abused Mr. Davitt as
he has abused the Bishop of Limerick, I should put my
name certainly. But I consider not himself, but what
he has written. It is a case of what I write against
what he writes. I seek to convince the public, not to
convert Mr. Davitt.

Now, let us proceed. This Primary Education Ques-
tion in England touches the Irish Catholics in England
almost exclusively. Now, what has their position been?
They had been paying the hare of the School Rate from
1870—which now has arisen in London to about 1s. 2d.
in the £1. Should they not, then, get a share in that
public money to which they were thus compelled to con-
tribute? That looks like common justice. But that
common justice was persistently denied them irom 1870
onwards  They had to build their own schools, keep
them in repair, pay their teachers, etc. They got a
share in the Government Grant; but in that the Board
Schools also shared; so I put that out of consideration.
But they paid their School Rates, and were demied a
penny’s help out of them. Those Rates were all con-
sumed in building palatial schools, equipping them, re-
pairing them, and paying teachers for the Nonconfor-
mists who, having never built schools for themselves,
fought for the Act of 1870, by which the public, in- g
cluding Irish Catholics in England, were made to supply h
schools for their convenience. Thus the Irish Catliolic :
had to build his own schools, and equip them in private
contributions out of his own pocket, and also pay his
share in Rates to provide schools to suit the Noncon-
formist. T have nothing to say as between tho Angli-
can and the Nonconformist. Let them fight their own
quarrels. T own I have more sympathy with the Non-'
conformist. Tfe is more consistent in general. Bus it
is curious that Mr. Davitt’s humanitarian heart goes
out to the English Nonconformist whilst he ignores the
injustice thus done to the Inish Catholic in England.

Mr. Davith says that it is not just that the Noncon-
formist should have to pay for the religious education o
of Catholics in schools. If he knew anything about the i
question, he would know that they were never asked to
do so. The case of the Catholics has always been— If
our schools give the public full value in secular oduca-
tion, subject to public inspection, why not pay our
schools for that educational value we give—especially
since the mouey is our own, which we have paid as
School Rates? "Give us just cnough for that secular
education which our schools impart, and we ask no
more. As to the religious training given in our schools,
we see to that ourselves, and pay for it without
troubling or taxing the public.”” ~ Of course, Mr.

injustice to the Irish Catholics in England. He does

Mr. Davitt called the Bishop of Limerick the “ hench-

_ dependent ; whatever those who dare to contradict him

Mr. Davitt says— Before the Act of 1902 was passed
the English Catholic schools were absolutely indepen-

! dent in management, and in every other respect. They
i obtained Parliamentary Grauts in proportion to ascer-
) tained educational results, etc. This state of
things was not satisfactory to the English Catholic
| leaders. They wanted more public money—not more
religious teaching—for their schools, and to obtain it
tliey resolved not only to support the Balfour Education
¢ - Bill of 1902, but to join their forces with those of the
Church of England against the party and people stand-
ing for the old School Board system. In doing this,
they consented to give away for more money the pre-
vious independence of Catholic schools, which had ob-
tained substantial public support.” I want to make a
{ commentary on that.

T observe:—1. The Catholics claimed for their schools
share in the School Rates, which they paid, as well as
‘the Nonconformists. To get that, they were prepared
to accept the help of any party; which was just as
natural as that Mr. Davitt allies himself with the Non-
conformists from whom he expects Flome Rule. T
would accept Home Rule in the morning from Tories,
Liberals, Radicals, or any other party; and I would
accept educational justice for Catholics similarly. The
“Tories happened to be able to help them in the matter,

it just as willingly from the Liborals. But the Liberals
never offered it. It may be that the Libem]s‘wouvld
‘make’ special exception for the consciences of Catholics
__and some of them certainly would. The Catholics
possibly made a mistake in not waiting to sce; and pos-
sibly they were wise in not running the ms)f. They
ook what came from those who gave it ; t_hab is all.

9. It is not true that they yielded their old frpcdom

4o teach religion in their schools for the share in the
Rates which they got in 1902. .They‘ have their
_yeligious emblems, prayers, Catechism, just as be-
fore, although it is true that they had to
yield, to some extent, 'thelr former independence
of management of their schools.  Because the
‘public claimed to be represented on the management
gince the schools were to share the Rates. For myself,

.the Catholics for allowing popular representation in the

management of their schools—not very consistent, cer-

3. He upbraids them with ‘“wanting more public
money,”’ whereas they already “ obtained Parliamen-
tary Grants.”” They wanted simply their rights—they
paid School Rates, and they claimed a share in them.
Mr. Davitt’s narrow view of their position reminds me
of what a person lately said to me about Home Rule.
He said:— Iave you not already got a lot of civil
liberty in Ireland?’ My veply was:—“It is not a
question of what amount of civil liberty we have got;
it is a question of the national rights we claim.”” T
daresay that Mr. Davitt would not approve the logie by
which my friend would deny Home Rule to Ireland;
but by what consistency, then, does he approve the logic
by which he denies educational justice to his fellow-
countrymen in England? Their schools had “already
obtained substantial public support,” and he thinks
they should grin and bear injustice for the rest. ~ They
simply claimed rights for cash paid, and they wanted to
get those rights. Those rights the Nonconformists
deny them, and to help the denial of those rights they
raise a catch-cry, in which Mr. Davitt lustily warbles
in the Nonconformist chorus.

4. He says, and with a sneer in his words, that the
Catholics were not satisfied with the Parliamentary
Grants which their schools had been getting. =~ Why
should they be satisfied? Those Parliamentary Grants
were shared in by all public Elementary Schools in com-
mon with Catholic schools. But there was the School
Rate besides. The Catholics paid their share of that,
and they claimed their just share of it. Why not?
one would say. But Mr. Davitt’s reply is, to cast ridi-
cule on the claim, and even to insinuate bad faith in
making the: claim.  He says:—“They wanted more
public money—not more religious teaching—for the'r
schools.”” They wanted what they had a right to—a
share in the Rates they were made to pay. That is
answer enough to Mr. Davitt’s unbecoming aspersions.
But let me add this also.

The Parliamentary Grant of which Mr. Davitt speaks
could not be got unless the manager of a Catholic school
could show that he had provided an equivalent sum
from private sources. That sum the manager had to
beg or borrow—his providing it, anyhow, was a
condition of his getting even the Parliamentary Grant
for his school. I have had personal knowledge of the
difficulties, straits, and worries of managers to scrape
that sum together. They had to provide the original
cost of building the schools, on which borrowed money
for building and ground rent they had a large interest
woighing on their shoulders. With all these they were
constantly in debt, many of them blessing their stars
if they could keep from sinking deeper in debt as the
years went on. Mr. Davitt says that they did not waus
more religion, but more money. Now, they could not
have the religion in the schools unless they had the
schools to have it in; and they could not have the
schools without the money to maintain them.  Mr.
Davitt cannot be so obtuse as not to see how that works
out. We are living on earth, not in heaven. But Mr.
Davitt talks ag if he thinks that Catholic teachers should
live on the wind, and teach for nothing. The mana-

rest of the island, I know that I am running

acting in direct contravention of the ideas of Davis and
all those who look upon every man born in this island
as an actual or potential Irishman. Were such a state-
ment made of those of alien race, who dwell in the
South and West of Ireland, I should be inclined to
agree with it. It is inconceivable that were Ireland
free the Protestants of the South could long hold out
in opposition. In so far as they are kept apart from
their fellow-countrymen, it is c¢/ass hatred that keeps
them so, and in a free and well-governed country class
hatred is not an enduring force. It gives way before
the first national crisis. Had Ireland got Home Rule
in 1885, the Parnell Split would probably have seen the
Southern Irish Protestant fighting sido by side with
the Irish Catholic.
* With the North, in my opinion, the case is different.
Here it is a matter of race distinction and race hatred.
There is no good-humoured dallying with the Irish lan-
guage in this part of the world; there aroc no devolu-
tionists. Nothing could, in my mind, really illustrate
the difference between the Northern and Southern Pro-
testant better than Lord Dunraven’s movement and Mr.
Sloan’s. Lord Dunraven’s is a movement of persons
who are prevented by their class interest from giving
full play to national sympathy. They love Ireland,
but not Michael Davitt. Sloan’s, on the contrary, is a
movement of men who are hindered in giving full play
to their democratic sympathies by their desire to pre-
serve their racial freedom and integrity. Michael
Davitt would be dear to them if he were not an Irish-
man. No doubt, the exceptional position of a subject
community sometimes brings the two parties into an
unnafural alliance, but in a free country the Sloanites
and the devolutionists would be natural: enemies.

And both parties refuse to admit any evidence to con-
travert the general propositioii. Now, I think that the
chances of our absorbing Ulster under Home Rule, and
of England absorbing us if we do not get it, are about
equally great. - It may be done, but it will take some
doing, aud just men will prefer not to sco that doing.

and with which we have no sympathy, a system which

the patriot, a system under which it is impossible to dis-
regard the fact that religion, unless sterilised by com-
plaisancy, is a ground of exclusion from office.

And fhis is precisely what the East Ulster Unionists
dread.  They are not Irish, and they don’t want to
become Irish, nor to suffer for not becoming so. I do
not believe it is really so much a desire for Irish plunder.
The bulk of that Ulster body, alike the Sloanite and
the Presbyterian, get but little of the plunder. Tt is
the middleman of Unionism—the Dublin gang—that
comes in for most of it. It is the dread of race absorp-
tion, added to that of religious domination, that really
moves the North, and makes them fervent in their op-
position to any.scheme of Home Rule. That they are

They have adopted precisely the same methods of pro-
pagating their opinion that we have of ours. The
Press, the platform, and the ballot-box have all borne

if these persons demand their national indepen-.
dence, and if they are, in truth, distinct in nationality,

I do not think that either the fact of dwelling on con-
fiscated Irish soil, or the fact that their freedom must
involve the continuance in subjection of about a hun-
dred thousand persons, is sufficient reason for such a
denial.

Again, even to those who seek a United Ireland it
seems to me that paradox though it be the separation
of East Ulster is the best way to attain that ideal if it
be at all possible. We are wont to use the argument
that Home Rule will mean a United Empire.  The
freedom of Ulster will very possibly bring about a simi-
lar result. In modern times a small minority is
genorally much better treated than a large one. The
Catholics in Trinity College get on excellently because
they are so few. The Protestants in Ireland and the
Catholics in Ulster would fare well, I believe, for this
very reason. The Catholics in Ulster would very prob-
ably have the favourable position of being the make-
weight in the denominational scale between the Church
of Ireland and the Presbyterians. There would thus
be no longer any Catholic and Protestant bitterness,
and after a time as Irish industries developed, and the
North and South became more economically one, it is
not impossible that East Ulster might, of its own free
will, become united-to Ireland. It is evident how much
more desirable a union under those circumstances

benefits of insurance were not so well understood then

AN ACTOR AND SOME REFLECT(ONS.

O outward appearance, at all events, the Irish scorn

of English drama is not nearly so :strong as the

Trvish scorn of English literature.. The.patronage be-
stowed upon the various “* Girl ”’ pieces needs.no telling.
To our credit be it said, however, that we are capable
of higher flights, and the attendances at the recent Ben-
son performances may stand as proof.; There was a
time when Mr. Benson could draw no more than a very
moderate-sized audience. Time, ‘however, told in his
favour, just as it tells against the flashy pretender to:
histrionic excellence. It came to be recognised that
there was a great all-round measure of merit in his pro-
ductions of Shakespearean plays, and that a better un-
derstanding of the play might be had from witnessing
one of his revivals than from watching the doings of star
actors who produce one of the great dvamatist’s works
with sundry startling innovations of interpretation, all
meant to augment the notoriety of the star, rather than
to make Shakespeart more intelligible. Mr. Benson
never worked on the star method. He sometimes took
a comparatively minor part if he thought he had a better

“man for the leading one, and he was ever caveful to have

the minor parts decently filled, a thing which was not
always so in the older days. Formerly Mr. Benson had
certain rather awkward peculiavities of voice and man-

er, which somehow militated almost as heavily against
him, as his scholarly thoughtfulness, thoroughness, and
refinement, told in his favour. These histrionic blem-
ishes grew worse, rather than better, up to some few
years ago. For instance, at the time he produced “ An-
tony and Cleopatra ’ they were at about their worst,
and they were then so pronounced that had they grown
much worse he would have been almost unendurable—
in his bad moments, at all events. Now, however, he
has got over all that, and has won such control over the
original harshnesses in his voice and gestures as practi-
cally emancipates him from his former drawbacks, and
enables one to enjoy his really fine conceptions without
any sense of jarring elements. For all this, Mr. Benson
is not a really great actor. He is defective on the lower
side of what makes for greatness in an actor. Ho is
deficient in the mere histrionic outfit—physique, vocal
energy, and, above all, in what I shall denominate ani-
mal fervour. He has, indeed, no fervour. He never
-has that note of passion in his voice which thrills the
hearer, whether he will or no; he never has any of that
animal energy, impulse, and “ go ’ which are the only
things that will convince us at times. Anything that
psychological insight can do, Mr. Benson is equal to; ne
can adumbrate the inmost thoughts of a character with
rare skill. But when it comes to representing those
things which are the result of fundamental passion and
not of thought at all, he falls short. I do not say he
really fails: he simply falls short, or carries only imper-
fect conviction. His rages and furies are worked up.
He can show what we may call “ cold *’ rage well enough,
as a rule; but at depicting those outbursts which are the
result of sudden provocation, and which take the very
subject of them himself by surprise, he is greatly defi-
cient. As Hamlet rebuking his mother in the closet
scene he is good ; what he totally lacks is that force of
passion which Salvini used to put into the great scenes
in his Othello. In a word, Mr. Benson is defective in
the big, elemental, unconscious qualities, without which
no actor can be entirely great. Were I a sort of sove-
reign magician, I think I could make a great actor by a
process of selection. I would take the physique of Barry
Sullivan, and some other parts of his histrionic equip-
ment, too; to these I would add the digmty of Booth,
the voice of Forbes Robertson, and the passionate qua-
lities of Salvini. To all T would add the joint minds of
Irving and Mr. Benson—together, no doubt, with bits
out of the other men’s brain-pans, too. The whole
should make a rare actor; how I wish we had him!
But if Mr. Benson, like the late Sir Henry Irving, is
deficient in the merely physiological outfit, he has com-
pensations. There are things—a few things—that your
““ robustious periwig-pated fellow *’ can do, and many
other things that he cannot do. Tt is in these latter
things that men like Benson have their innings.  The
old robust school shouted through the play, and filled
you with a momentary wonderment at what masterful
actors they were; they never led you on to as much as
one new thought upon the play, or made you think
afresh what a great dramatist Shakespeare was. What-
ever they could really do, they made that blaze; they
tore through the rest indifferently, and slurred over
many a delicately luminous point in the lines they had
to deliver. Tndeed very often they simply elocuted
‘them, rather than delivered them as artists.
Very often, too, their shouting qualities con-
cealed their lack of true feeling, so that “ declama-
tion roared and passion slept.”” They spoilt many a
elicate thing, and their blatant emphasis of effect grated
upon the trained observer keenly; in short, they often
““made the judicious grieve.”” Certainly with Mr. Ben-
son that is not so; he has no blatancies, and never does
anything that grates upon one’s taste. It may occa-
sionally be possible to dissent from his interpretations,
‘but never to pronounce them vulgar or crude. Indeed T
+tEink that on the whole, the performances of his com-
pany form about the best commentary upon Shakespeare
‘that one could have. Each performance is a deeply-
‘considered unfolding of the play, not a series of star-

In saying thus much about Mr. Benson, I hope I have
incidentally brought out a certain leading truth, which
concerns closely all who think that they were destined
for the boards. This truth is that the physical outfit
of the actor is not everything, .tvhou_gh a fine physical
cquipment, both vocal and bodily, is necessary. to the
making of a great actor. Unless there is a deep fund
of organic cnergy and natural impulse to draw upon,
your actor will hardly make the most lasting impression.
But bodily fitness is not all in the making of the actor,
for unless he “ have that within which passes show ”” he
can never rank among the Garricks and Talmas. The
great, even the good, actor must be something of a
scholar and a man of culture, so that he may rightly
understand what he is to interpret. He must be some-
thing of a philosopher too, and a man of the world. for
since he is to present men he must observe at first hand
what he is to present. In short, he must bo like all
other artists who can lay any claim to merit—he must
bring fresh thought and observation to bear on his art.

F the alleged letter of Erasmus Smith be not genuine,
it cannot prove anything on either side. If it be
geuuine, I have shown in the last article that it proves
in Erasmus Smith’s own words that he was compelled by
Charles 11. to impose on his endowment all the Episco-
palian conditions or restrictions that were imposed on
1t by the Charter, the bye-laws, and the rules estab-
~ lished, that is, all the Episcopalian Protestant condi-
tions that were imposed on it; and that he ““ could not
have helped ** imposing and observing those conditions.
So that the meaning of his words: “ My end in found-
ing the three schools was to propagate the Protestant
faith according to the Scriptures, avoiding all supersti-
tion, as the Charter, and the bye-laws, and the rules
<stablished doe direct,”’ s, “ My end in founding the
three schools was to propagate the Protestant faith ac-
cording to the Scriptures, avoiding all superstition, as
His Majesty has commanded ’*; that his end was the
end which His Majesty had commanded. And, hence,
even though the words “ for to remove that barre is to
1 make them Seminaries of Popery ’ were genuine, they
{ would prove nothing as to Erasmus Smith’s intention ;
I- for His Majesty, who had commanded him to make his
schools Seminaries of Protestantism, would not have
allowed him to make them  Seminaries of Popery.’
Since, then, this letter of 1682, if genuine, proves up
to the hilt the whole case against Episcopalian Protes-
tants, it may be asked, in wonder, how could they seek
to derive any advantage from it? How could they have
made it-—as they have——their Magna Charta? — They
have done so by the mcst shameless use of garbled ex-
tracts entirely misrepresenting the writer’s meaning.
Erasmus Smith has told us that his end in founding
the three schools was the end which His Majesty had
2 commanded. But Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, Rep. 1894,
p- 236, and the Rev. Mr. Todd Martin, Moderator, etc.,
Rep. 1891-2, p. 177, quote Erasmus Smith as saying:
“ My end in founding the three schools was to propagate
the Protestant faith, according to the Scriptures, avoid-
ing all superstition,”” dishonestly leaving out that part
of the quotation which states that he had chosen that
end because His Majesty had commanded it. They thus
represent him as stating that what he has so definitely
told us was the end of His Majesty, King Charles II.,
was his own end. And the Lord Justice has used this
quotation again and again.
Lord Justice Fitzgibbon is not over particular about
proof when he wants to establish a position. He wanted
. to establish the position that the Masters and Trustees
2 were inclined to admit Catholic children to the schools
without observing the Protestant rules, and he gives two
- quotations from the E. S. Governors’ Minute Book
£y which prove the very opposite.
. The first Minute states that Elisha Coles, Master of
e Galway School, should be directed not to exclude “ the
children of Papists, so that they conform to the rules of
the School.”” This proves that Elisha was keeping the
f Catholic children out instead of letting them in.,
l ¢ In the second Minute, the Trustees tell Erasmus
0 Smith that except the Catholic schools are soon closed,
his charity and cost expended on the schools will be
rendered ineffectual. Rep. 1894, p. 238. Thus the
Trustees have called for the suppression of the Catholic
schools, and not for the suppression of the Protestant
rules in their own schools. From these two Minutes,
the Lord Justice concludes that the Trustees wrote to
Eragmus Smith asking him to allow them to admit Ca-
tholic children to their schools, without observing the
rotestant rules; and he says that the letter of the Gth
" June, 1682, was a reply to their request. But in
ho first paragraph of the letter Evasmus says that he

had written the letter before he had received their re-
port. It was not, therefore, a reply to the report. And
ho has not adduced a particle of evidence to show that
the Trustees had asked Erasmus Smith to relax the Pro-
testant rules in the schools. Moreover, we may b.e cer-
tain that the Trustecs never asked Erasmus Smith to
relax those rules, for such a request would be to ask him
to break the law. i

Tho Lord Justice, after quoting the letter in full,
says:—If we are bound by Erasmus Smith’s para-
mount intention, and if we have to determine whether
the desire to inculcate Protestantism, or to teach f/he
tenants’ children must prevail, how could the question
ba more sharply put, or the answer be more lemphatll-
cally given, than in that letter? He states his end in
founding the three schools (o propagate the Protestan'
faith, he insists upon his own rules, and he sppporfs
re that they may be observed by giving it
as the command of His Majesty that the children shall
be catechised, and have the Catechism expounded to
them. If parents will exclude their children because
prayers, Catechism, and exposition are commanded, he
cannot help it, and he prefers to sce the schools “ con-
sumptive ”’ rather than to “ make them Seminaries of
Popery by removing the bar of compulsory Protestant
teaching. Finally, he commands the newly-appointed
Master to observe those duties, and the Governors to
expel them that decline those duties.”” The italics are
the Lord Justice’s. 3

In the whole rang> of controversy there is not a greater
perversion of a passage than this. Leaving out the last
sentence, every statement of fact in it is false, except
one which contains a half truth and a whole falsehood,
and this is the statement to which the Lord Justice has
called attentian by putting it in italics. It is:—“ He
states his end in founding the three schools fo propagate
the Protestan! faith.”” Erasmus Smith has stated that
his end in founding the threa schools was to propagate
the Protestant faith; and so far the words of the Lord
Justice express a truth, but only a half truth; for Eras-
mus Smith has told us that he had adopted that end
because His Majesty had commanded it. And, hence,
when the Lord Justice tells us, as he has told us, that
Erasmus Smith had adopted that end of his own free
will, and independently of any command of His Majesty,
he expresses a whole falsehood. And here again, by a
garbled quotation, and leaving out the words in which
Erasmus Smith tells why he had chosen that end, he
states the very opposite to what Erasmus Smith has said.
namely, he states that Erasmus Smith had freely and
spontaneously chosen that end. This statement is abso-
lutely false. The Lord Justice says:— He insists upon
his own rules.”” Erasmus Smith has told us in the letter
that the rules are not /s own rules, but the express com-
mands of His Majesty. He has told us that His Majesty
has not only commanded the end, but also the means by
which the end is to be attained, namely, “ the Charter,
the bye-laws and the rules established.”

The Lord Justice says:-—“ And he supports his own
desire that they may be observed, by giving it as the
command of His Majesty, that the children shall be
catechised, and have the Catechism expounded to them.’”
Here the Lord Justice has surpassed himself. Erasmus
Smith has told us that His Majesty had commanded to
catechise the children out of Ussher’s Catechism, and
ho expresses his own “ humble desire *’ that the King’s
command may be observed. The Lord Justice has
turned this passage upside down. He has told us that
the rule to catechise the children out of Ussher’s Cate-
chism originated with Erasmus Smith, and that he
merely called in the King as a sort of helper!

The Lord Justice tells us that “ ho’’ (Erasmus Smith)y
“ prefers to see his schools ‘ consumptive * rather than
to * make them Seminavies of Popery ’ by removing the
bar of compulsory Protestant teaching.”’

I have proved that the clause of the letter whicl
spoke of the “ Seminavies of Popery,’” was interpolated,
and was never written by Erasmus Smith. I have showi
that even if the clause were genuiue, Erasmus Smith
had no choice in the matter, as the King, who had com-
manded that the schools should be made Seminarics of
Protestantism, would not have allowed them to be made

- Seminaries of Popery.

FEBRUARY 17, 1906:

I have shown that Erasmus
Smith has told us that he had no choice or  preference
in the matter, because prayers, Catechism and exposi-
tion were commanded by His Majesty and could not be
changed by him. So that when the Lord Justice says
that Erasmus Smith prefers to see his schools “ consump-
tive,” that is nearly empty, rather than remove the bar
of compulsory Protestant teaching, he is drawing on his
imagination.

Tho attempt, therefore, which the Lord Justica has
made to prove from this letter that Erasmus Smith’s
paramount intention was to inculcate Protestantism
rather than to teach the tenants’ children, gets no sus-
tainment. whatever from this letter. For Erasmus Smith
has told us that all the Protestantism of the schools
had been commanded by the King, and that he was
powerless to change it. = The issue, therefore, whether
he preferred to inculcate Protestantism, or to leave out
Protestantism and teach the tenants’ children, could not
have been raised, because one alternative of that issue,
namely, Protestantism, was fixed and immovable.
Therefore, the issue was not “sharply put,”” nor was
““ the answer emphatically given.”” The issue was in no
way raised, and, consequently, in no way answered.

Of the letter the Lord Justice says:— The whole
case of the Grammar Schools is there in a nutshell.

" Lay aside every consideration of the religious inequality,

and the szctarian legislation of that day, and those who
cannot and will not accept instruction upon the Foun-
der’s conditions, cannot now have any moral or equit-
able claim.to the benefit of his endowment which they
could not then have to enter his schools against his will.”

The whole fallacy of the Lord Justice is here in a
nutshell. He assumes that the Protestant conditions
“ commanded ”’ by His Majesty, as “ the Charter, and
the bye-laws, and the rules established doe direct,’’ were
the “ Founder’s conditions,”” although the Founder has
told us distinctly, that they were not his conditions, but
the conditions of His Majesty. He assumes that the
“ sectarian legislation *’ mmanded ”’ by Iis Ma-
iesty were the “ conditions ontaneously imposed by
Erasmus Smith,. although Erasmus Smith has expressly
told us that those ““ conditions’’ had been imposed by
the King, and that he could not change them. Eras-

against the will and “ command ”’ of His Majesty. He
has told us that he had no will in the matter, because

cnter the schools against the will of Erasmus Smith, is

DEAR SIR—Would you kindly insert the following

remarks in your valuable paper? I think they
may interest some of my countrymen at home. I often
hear it said that our Ivish cattle-feeders and dealers are
very much handicapped by the present method of tran-
sit of their fat cattle to Glasgow market, and to the
English markets.  Training, shipping, and the cross-
Chaunel voyage causes a depreciation in value to the
extent of ten shillings per hundred live weight in fat
cattle landed here from Ireland. That, in an ordinary-
size beast, which weighs eight hundred or thereabouts,
would amount to the large sum of four pounds. This,
uo doubt, is a considerable loss to Irish feeders.

When deploring it one day to a gentleman here, he
told me that the loss could be easily obviated by
slaughtering the fat cattle in Treland, sewing up the car-
casses in canvas, and sending them across. And thus
the transit, apart altogether from the depreciation in
value, would be rendered casier. He pointed out,
moreover, that the hides, horns and tallow would help
to build up new industries at home, and furnish lucra-
tive employment for some of those who are at present
forced to emigrate.  Here he quoted a case in point

the little town of Crieff, in Perthshire.

The cattle-feeders in this locality realised that they
were at a loss in sending their live stock to the large
markets in Great Britain. Somo enterprising indivi-
duals in the locality resolved to remedy this evil, and
their efforts were very successful. X

Buyers go about tKe country who buy the fat cattle
direct of the feeders. Tho fat cattle are slaughtered
in the little town of Crieff, and the carcasses are sent
to the London markets. I was informed on good autho-
vity that the little town of Crieffl derives one million
pounds per annum from its cattle trade.

Coincident with this, there are other flourishing in-
d\\s:x‘lcs-uamely. a tannery, where the hides of the
cattle ave tanned; a comb-making industry from the
horns, ete., and a soap-making industry from tho tallow,

to make this not very long ago insignificant village a
very flourishing little town, the population of which
was in 1901 5,200.

Such industries would be badly needed in Ireland to
help to stem the tide of emigration. I hope my coun-
trymen at home will give this example of enterprise,
although it be from Scotchmen, a little consideration.—
Yours faithfully, 0. B.

ON THE WARPATH.

A Leaderite found himself in Waterford recently—
in Waterford where even the soap in the hotels is Bri-
tish—and being in the humour for enquiry he rambled
into a big shop to buy some collars.

“I want a collar, same shape as I am wearing, size
g,

Boxes pulled down, and the correct article discovered.

“Of course, these are Irish,”’ quietly observed the
purchaser.

‘“Oh, yes,”” promptly, and thoughtlessly answered
the assistant.

“You seem very cocksure about it. How do you
happen to know?’ said the purchaser, as he examined
the collars and the box and found nothing whatever to
show that the collars were Irish.

“Well, I think they are, anyhow,”’ said the smart
boy when he found the class of man he had.

“ You shouldn’t be thinking. You should not say a
thing is Irish unless you know it is,”” thundered the
Leaderite, now getting warm.

“I beg your pardon, sir, wait till I speak to the
“buyer,” ”’ said the lad tremblingly, and he ran down
and interviewed somebody behind a pile, and returned
quickly.

“The ‘buyer’ says they are made in Londonderry,
Sind

“Why isn’t the Londonderry maker’s name on the
box, or on the collar?”’

“T don’t know.”’

“Well, I think 7 know,” and the Leaderite raised
his voico so that it would be heard by the gentleman
behind the kopje, whose ear was cocked now. Tell
your buyer to get his friends in Londonderry to stamp
their name on the collars in future if he wishes for my
custom.”’

The next shop tried was , and here collars stamped
“Irish manufacture ’’ were produced without Irish col-
lars being asked for, and the purchaser was satisfied,
but when paying he gave the young man an account of
his previous experience, and said, “ If you had shown
me English collars first I would have left the shop.”
